title: Chapter 37: The Interview
content: Eighteen months.

Eighteen months since I chose. Since I stood at the fork — three paths, three lives, three versions of everything I could be — and picked this one. This café. This village. This man who is currently standing in the kitchen doorway with a dish towel over his shoulder, watching me pace.
"Avery."
"I'm fine."
"You're wearing a groove in the floor."
"I'm fine."
"You've said that eleven times since breakfast."

I stop pacing. Close my eyes. The floorboards creak beneath my feet — old wood, warm from the morning sun cutting through the kitchen window. I can smell the coffee Julian made, dark and rich, the kind that curls into the corners of the room and stays. Gatsby is perched on the windowsill, cleaning his paws with the particular arrogance of a cat who has never once been nervous about anything.
"You're not nervous about the interview," Julian says. It's not a question.
"I can do interviews. Wall Street trained me for interviews. I once talked a Bloomberg reporter out of a hatchet piece by explaining collateralized debt obligations until his eyes glazed over."
Julian grins. "So what's the problem?"

The problem is that a journalist from The Irish Times is coming to write a profile on Paws & Pause. Our café. The café that started as a hiding place and became — somehow, impossibly — a community hub.

The problem is that journalists dig. And Julian's past — the real past, the one where he was a fugitive framed for corporate crimes he didn't commit — that past is supposed to be over. His name is cleared. The records are sealed. But "cleared" and "forgotten" are not the same thing, and I know how a single question can crack open a door you spent years learning to close.
"She might ask about you," I say. "About before."
Julian shrugs. One shoulder, casual, the way he does when he's being deliberately nonchalant. "My name is cleared. Everything else is just a story."
"Just a story," I repeat.

He crosses the kitchen, dish towel and all, and puts his hands on my shoulders. His thumbs press into the tension knots at the base of my neck, and I hate how well he knows my body — every stress point, every holding place for anxiety, every spot that turns me back into putty.
"The truth is, Avery, I don't care what she writes about me. I care about what she writes about this place. About what we built."
We. The word is small and enormous at the same time.
"What time is she coming?" I ask.
"Noon. You have forty-five minutes to wear out the floor."
I throw a dish towel at him. He catches it, because he catches everything, and because the universe has a sense of humor about the things I throw.

Siobhán Kelly arrives at noon exactly, which I respect. She's younger than I expected — early thirties, maybe, with dark curly hair pulled back in a clip and the kind of attentive eyes that make you feel like you're the only person in the room. She's wearing a green corduroy jacket and carrying a leather satchel that looks like it's been everywhere. She orders an Americano before she even sits down, which is how I know she's going to be fair.

We do the interview at the corner table by the window — the one where the light is best, where the café looks its warmest. Mika helped me stage it last night: fresh wildflowers in the ceramic vase Julian made in his pottery phase (three weeks, two mugs, one very lopsided bowl), the menu chalkboard scrubbed clean, Gatsby settled on his designated windowsill like a furry gargoyle.

Siobhán takes out a notebook. Not a recorder — a notebook. A real one, with a pen that scratches across the paper in quick, curling strokes. The sound of it takes me somewhere old, somewhere I don't expect: the scratch of a pen in a conference room, the notepad in my hand during quarterly earnings calls. The life I used to have. The sound is the same, but everything else is different.
"So," she says, smiling. "Paws & Pause. It's a wonderful name. Tell me how it started."
She looks between us, but Julian is the one who speaks. He leans back in his chair, one arm draped over the back of mine, his fingers resting lightly on my shoulder. Casual. Anchored.
"Avery found me when I was at my lowest," he says. "The café gave me a reason to stay."

His voice is steady. No performance in it, no rehearsal — just the honest architecture of a story told often enough to be true. But there's weight in the word "found." He means it the way you mean a thing that changed your life. He means it the way you mean the moment everything tilted.

And I realize, sitting there with the coffee smell and the pen scratching and the June light falling warm across the table, that this is the true version. Not the version where I shifted through realities, where a glitch in an app deposited me in a version of Ireland I wasn't supposed to reach. That happened. That's real. But it's not the truth anymore.

The truth is simpler: I found him. I stayed. The café gave him a reason. The multiverse part isn't the truth of how we live now. The choosing part is. And maybe that's what truth always was — not what happened, but what you keep choosing after.
Siobhán's pen scratches on. "And you kept it going together? Through all the challenges of a new business?"
"We argued about everything," I say. "The menu. The paint color. Whether we should have a cat policy."
"Who won the cat policy debate?"
Julian and I look at each other. Then we both look at Gatsby, who is currently licking his paw without a care in any universe.
"He won," Julian says.
Siobhán laughs, and it's a real laugh — surprised, unguarded. She writes something down, still smiling.
Then she turns to me. Her eyes focus, and I feel the shift: this is the question she's been building to.
"You moved from New York to rural Ireland. That's a dramatic change. What made you choose this life?"

And there it is. The question. The one that used to freeze me solid. The one that used to make the FOMO climb up my throat like a living thing, whispering: But what about the other lives? What about the paths you didn't take? What about—
But now? Right now, with Julian's fingers on my shoulder and Gatsby in the window and the smell of coffee and old wood and the pen waiting for my answer?
I don't hesitate.

"I stopped being afraid that I was missing something better." The words come out clear, certain — not rehearsed but lived, like a path walked so many times it's worn into the earth. "Because there isn't something better. There's just different. And I chose this different."
Siobhán's pen moves fast. She writes it down word for word — I can tell by the rhythm, the way her handwriting accelerates to capture something before it escapes.
Julian's fingers squeeze my shoulder. I look at him. He's grinning, and his lips form the word without sound: *Different.*
Same soul. Same humor. Same man who can take the heaviest moment of my life and make it lighter just by mouthing a single word at me across a table.
I bite the inside of my cheek so I don't laugh. Siobhán keeps writing. The interview goes on.

The article goes live that evening. The Irish Times works fast — faster than I expected, faster than the Wall Street publications I used to track with the seriousness of a wartime correspondent. By nine p.m., my phone is buzzing.
Mika's name flashes on the screen. Video call.
I answer, and her face fills the phone — dark hair tumbling out of a bun, reading glasses perched on her nose, the glow of a laptop screen turning her skin blue-white.
"You're quoted," she says, by way of hello.
"Hi to you too."
"'There isn't something better. There's just different.'" She lowers her glasses and peers at me through the screen. "That's the most Avery thing you've ever said."
I laugh, the kind that comes from the stomach, the kind that only Mika can pull out of me. "It took me three lifetimes to figure that out."

The laugh dies. We both freeze — me on the couch in Kilmog, her in her apartment in New York, connected by a signal that crosses an ocean and a truth that crosses something larger. Because it's technically true. Three lifetimes. Three paths. Three versions of Avery Chen who lived three different stories, and only one of them is sitting on this couch with a cat on her feet and a husband making tea in the kitchen.
Only Mika knows. The weight of that knowledge settles between us for exactly one second — a held breath, a shared glance through the screen.
Then Mika cracks up. Her whole face transforms, nose scrunching, glasses sliding down, the laugh that used to get us shushed in the NYPL.
"God, I wish I could put that on your tombstone." She wipes her eyes. "'It took me three lifetimes.' People would think it was metaphorical."
"It is metaphorical," I say, deadpan.
"Sure. And I'm the Queen of England."

We talk for another hour — about her new project, about the article, about whether she's coming for Christmas ("Yes, and I'm bringing real bagels, not whatever sad approximation you've been surviving on"). When we hang up, the apartment feels both quieter and fuller than before.

Julian makes dinner — pasta with fresh basil from the window box and more bread than two people need. He opens the article on his phone and reads it aloud, standing at the counter with a wooden spoon in one hand, doing voices.
"'Paws & Pause is a *transformative* space —'" He draws out the word, rolling each syllable like a marble in his mouth. "— 'a café that has become the beating heart of a small community.'"
"I like that she called it a beating heart."
"'Owner Julian Gallagher credits his wife —'" He pauses. Raises an eyebrow at me. "'Wife.' I like that word."
"Keep reading."

"'Owner Julian Gallagher credits his wife, Avery Chen, with the café's very existence, saying she found him at his lowest and gave him reason to stay.'" He reads it casually, but his voice softens on the last words, and he doesn't look up from the screen.

He skips the next paragraph — the one about his past, the clearing of his name, the "challenges" that Siobhán handles with careful, respectful phrasing. Then he lands on my quotes and reads them again.
"'There isn't something better. There's just different.'"
"Julian, you've read that part three times."

"I know." He looks up, and his eyes are soft the way they get when it's just us, when the performance drops and what's left is the thing underneath — the raw, unguarded thing I fell in love with across an impossible distance.
"Different," he says, tasting the word like it's something on a menu he might order again. "I like being your different."
I throw a piece of bread at him.
He catches it. Without looking. And eats it.
Married people are disgusting.

After dinner, the dishes are done and Julian is in the shower and the apartment is settling into its nighttime quiet — the creak of old beams, the hum of the refrigerator, Gatsby's purr from the armchair where he's curled into a perfect circle of smugness. I sit on the couch and look at my phone.

The article is beautiful. I've read it three times already, and each time I find something new to love — the way Siobhán describes the café's light, the texture she gives the village, the quiet respect in her handling of Julian's past. It's a real story about a real place, and for the first time, I see us from the outside. Two people who built something. Two people who chose.

And in the photo — oh, the photo. Siobhán's photographer caught us standing in front of the café, the green door behind us, the hand-painted sign above. Julian's arm around my waist. My head tipped back mid-laugh, caught in the exact moment before the sound. And Gatsby, because of course, photobombing from the windowsill with half his face visible, one amber eye catching the camera like he planned the whole thing.
Two people who found each other across impossible distances and chose to stand still.
My phone screen goes dark.
And in that moment — that hairline, fraction-of-a-second moment between the light and the dark — I see it.
The Fork app icon.

Three paths. Three choices. That simple geometric design I haven't seen in eighteen months, that I've tried to forget, that lives somewhere in the architecture of my brain like a room I've locked and lost the key to.
Then the screen goes dark for real. Black glass. My own faint reflection staring back at me, wide-eyed, in the dark.
I stare at it. My thumb hovers over the screen, not pressing, just — hovering. Waiting.
Nothing.

The screen stays dark. The app store has no record of anything called Fork. There is no icon, no glitch, no multiverse menu hovering at the edge of my vision. There hasn't been, not since the night I chose.

Maybe it was a ghost. An afterimage burned into my retinas, the way you see a camera flash after it goes off. Maybe it was a memory — the kind so deeply embedded that it surfaces without warning, without permission, in the spaces between one moment and the next.
Or maybe it was the universe winking.
Not pulling me back. Not offering a new choice. Just... acknowledging. *I was here. I changed you. And sometimes, in the dark, you'll still feel the shape of me.*
I put the phone down on the couch cushion, facedown, so the screen can't show me anything at all.

From the bathroom, I hear Julian singing — something off-key and half-remembered, the way he always sings in the shower, the way I'll hear him do it tomorrow and the day after and the day after that. Gatsby lifts his head, yawns, and resettles into his circle. The apartment smells like basil and coffee and the lavender detergent Julian buys because he knows I like it.
I get up. I go to bed with my husband and our cats and our life — our single, beautiful, chosen timeline — and I do not look at my phone again.
The Fork app is not coming back. I know that the way I know my own heartbeat: deeply, certainly, without question.
But it was real. And it left a mark. And sometimes — just sometimes, in the dark spaces between one world and the next — the mark flickers.
And that's enough. That's more than enough.
That's everything.
